transcript.speaker: HOST_A
transcript.text: Welcome back to Nous. Today we are breaking down photosynthesis.

transcript.speaker: HOST_B
transcript.text: Oh nice. I have wondered about this one for ages,

transcript.speaker: HOST_A
transcript.text: Right. And since we had no leftover questions from last time, we get a totally fresh start.

transcript.speaker: HOST_B
transcript.text: Perfect. So give me the one line version. What is it really?

transcript.speaker: HOST_A
transcript.text: At its core, photosynthesis turns sunlight into chemical energy.

transcript.speaker: HOST_B
transcript.text: Umm, so the plant is basically eating light?

transcript.speaker: HOST_A
transcript.text: Exactly. The plant captures light and stores it as food.

transcript.speaker: HOST_B
transcript.text: Okay. But how does a leaf even grab something like light?

transcript.speaker: HOST_A
transcript.text: Great question. That is where chlorophyll comes in.

transcript.speaker: HOST_A
transcript.text: Chlorophyll is the green pigment inside plant leaves.

transcript.speaker: HOST_B
transcript.text: So that is why leaves are green,

transcript.speaker: HOST_A
transcript.text: Right. And that pigment absorbs the light energy.

transcript.speaker: HOST_B
transcript.text: Wait, let me slow you down. Is chlorophyll like a tiny solar panel?

transcript.speaker: HOST_A
transcript.text: That is a perfect analogy. It soaks up sunlight, just like a panel.

transcript.speaker: HOST_B
transcript.text: Okay. Now I am picturing it. So what does the plant do with that energy?

transcript.speaker: HOST_A
transcript.text: It takes two simple ingredients. Water and carbon dioxide.

transcript.speaker: HOST_B
transcript.text: Water from the soil, and the gas from the air,

transcript.speaker: HOST_A
transcript.text: Exactly. And it transforms them into glucose and oxygen.

transcript.speaker: HOST_B
transcript.text: So glucose is the food, and oxygen is the leftover?

transcript.speaker: HOST_A
transcript.text: Right. The glucose feeds the plant. The oxygen gets released for us.

transcript.speaker: HOST_B
transcript.text: Umm, that is a pretty good deal for us honestly,

transcript.speaker: HOST_A
transcript.text: It really is. Now this happens in two main stages.

transcript.speaker: HOST_B
transcript.text: Two stages. Okay, walk me through the first one.

transcript.speaker: HOST_A
transcript.text: The first stage is the light dependent reactions.

transcript.speaker: HOST_A
transcript.text: These happen inside the thylakoid membranes.

transcript.speaker: HOST_B
transcript.text: Whoa. Wait. That word is a mouthful. Thylakoid?

transcript.speaker: HOST_A
transcript.text: Ha, yes. Just think of them as tiny stacked disks inside the leaf.

transcript.speaker: HOST_B
transcript.text: Like little pancakes where the light gets captured,

transcript.speaker: HOST_A
transcript.text: Exactly. That is where light becomes usable energy.

transcript.speaker: HOST_B
transcript.text: Got it. And the second stage?

transcript.speaker: HOST_A
transcript.text: The second stage is called the Calvin cycle.

transcript.speaker: HOST_A
transcript.text: It happens in a region called the stroma.

transcript.speaker: HOST_B
transcript.text: And what is the Calvin cycle actually doing?

transcript.speaker: HOST_A
transcript.text: It fixes carbon. That means it locks carbon into sugar.

transcript.speaker: HOST_B
transcript.text: So stage one grabs the energy, and stage two builds the food?

transcript.speaker: HOST_A
transcript.text: Perfect summary. Energy first, then construction.

transcript.speaker: HOST_B
transcript.text: Okay. This is clicking now. But why should we care so much?

transcript.speaker: HOST_A
transcript.text: Because this process sustains nearly all life on Earth.

transcript.speaker: HOST_B
transcript.text: Umm, that feels like a big claim,

transcript.speaker: HOST_A
transcript.text: It is true though. It makes the oxygen we breathe.

transcript.speaker: HOST_A
transcript.text: And it forms the base of nearly every food chain.

transcript.speaker: HOST_B
transcript.text: So without plants doing this, the whole chain collapses,

transcript.speaker: HOST_A
transcript.text: Exactly. No food, and no air. It is that fundamental.

transcript.speaker: HOST_B
transcript.text: Okay. Let me try to recap the whole thing,

transcript.speaker: HOST_A
transcript.text: Go for it.

transcript.speaker: HOST_B
transcript.text: Chlorophyll grabs the light. Water and carbon dioxide come in.

transcript.speaker: HOST_B
transcript.text: Then we get glucose and oxygen out. Two stages make it happen.

transcript.speaker: HOST_A
transcript.text: That is spot on. You nailed it.

transcript.speaker: HOST_B
transcript.text: This actually makes so much sense now,

transcript.speaker: HOST_A
transcript.text: And that is photosynthesis. Thanks for learning with us.

transcript.speaker: HOST_B
transcript.text: See you all next time on Nous,

note: Audio could not be generated right now (the free text-to-speech service was unreachable): TTS failed for a line after 4 attempts: Cannot connect to host speech.platform.bing.com:443 ssl:<ssl.SSLContext object at 0x000001B462BD36B0> [getaddrinfo failed]. The transcript is still available.
